summary: Hotz argues that no technology today can survive without human maintenance, unlike even the simplest biological life. He proposes that truly solving AI means creating machines capable of self-sufficient survival, maintenance, and reproduction. Using his 123-acre land purchase as both inspiration and testing ground, he envisions a future 'silicon ecology' where machines can fight entropy, maintain themselves, and form an ecosystem. He sees comma.ai's self-driving technology as foundational infrastructure for this broader vision of autonomous machine life.
key_points: Unlike even single-celled organisms, no current technology can survive and reproduce independently of humans
Wozniak's Coffee Test (making coffee in an unfamiliar home) is harder than self-driving cars and represents a better AI benchmark
openpilot's infrastructure is universal enough to serve as the foundation for diverse forms of 'AI life'
Machines have advantages over biology: they can directly share learned weights and communicate via high-speed electromagnetic signals
True AI means self-sufficient survival and the ability to fight entropy using abundant solar energy
money_quotes: The smallest animal, even the smallest cell, is capable of survival and reproduction. To date, no technological system is.
You've seen zombie movies. It all falls fast.
Nature is winning. Technology without humans looks pathetic.
What would it take to build technology that can fight entropy? Leave it alone, and after 5 years it has built a paradise.
That's a harder problem than self driving cars.
This is the future. A silicon ecology. A symbiotic relationship instead of a codependent one.
While the universe globally tends toward higher entropy, locally we can use energy to fight back and maintain. And energy is abundant from the sun.
Self sufficient survival is the test. With time to spare to make it all nice for humans too.
key_insight: Solving AI isn't about passing benchmarks or matching human performance on tasks—it's about creating technology that can survive and thrive independently, fighting entropy the way biology does.
